---
title: "Make it your own: tips and tricks for effortless stand-out visualisations in R"

# Publication date of this entry
date: "2026-03-06"

event:
  date: "Thu 26 March, 2026"
  # time_start:
  # time_end:
  # time_zone:
  time: "12:00 pm - 1:00 pm"
  summary: "We are delighted to welcome invited speaker Cara Thompson on tips
    and tricks for effortless stand-out visualisations in R."
  # location: ""
  # location_url: ""
  online_only: true
  hybrid: true
  hybrid_url: "https://teams.microsoft.com/l/message/19:7067cf1044b243a4a548d19ef38dd4ca@thread.tacv2/1770044243310?tenantId=c152cb07-614e-4abb-818a-f035cfa91a77&groupId=dde76344-7a98-4c83-bfe6-4e06b718c013&parentMessageId=1770044243310&teamName=Computation%20and%20Data%20in%20Research%20(CaDiR)&channelName=R%20User%20Group%20(R.U.M.)&createdTime=1770044243310"
  meetup_url: "https://www.meetup.com/r-users-group-uni-manchester/events/313172815/"
# chair:

talk:
  speaker_1: "Cara Thompson"
  topic_1: "Make it your own: tips and tricks for effortless stand-out
    visualisations in R"

bio:
  speaker_1: |
    Cara is a data visualisation consultant based in Edinburgh. She helps
    data-driven organisations and research teams communicate their findings to
    their end users through compelling and intuitive data visualisations. Her
    services include a combination of training, dataviz toolkits such as bespoke
    R packages, and dataviz commissions. She is an active member of the online
    RStats community, and enjoys learning out loud and experimenting with new
    ideas. 

content:
  speaker_1: |
    This talk explores the impact of good colour and annotation choices on the
    readability of our graphs. We will start by making a basic ggplot graph, and
    set about styling it, applying good principles of dataviz and typography. At
    each step, I will show how to implement this in ggplot, and highlight the
    traps I have fallen into so that others can avoid them! The aim of the
    session is to equip users to make more of their visualisations in ggplot,
    and make the case for investing in doing this programmatically. All the code
    will be shared with participants for them to continue building on after the
    session.
---

<!-- *** Note *** -->
<!-- Dynamically constructed links: -->
<!-- 1. meta event.location-link is constructed in content/events/_construct-event-location.lua -->
<!-- 2. meta event.meetup-link is constructed in content/events/_construct-event-meetup.lua -->

[{{< meta event.summary >}}]{.event-summary} \
[{{< meta event.date >}} @ {{< meta event.time >}}]{.event-time} \
[{{< meta event.location-link >}}]{.event-location}

<!-- The following content will only be rendered if -->
<!-- meta event.has-meetup-link is true -->
<!-- Note: This is currently not working and is rendering in the opposite -->
<!-- way from what is expected ... so comment out the conditional logic for -->
<!-- now pending further investigation! (meta event.meetup-link will be an -->
<!-- empty string if the url is not provided) -->
<!-- ::: {.content-hidden unless-meta="event.has-meetup-link"} -->
[{{< meta event.meetup-link >}}]{.event-meetup}
<!-- ::: -->

**Talk**: *{{< meta talk.topic_1 >}}*

{{< meta content.speaker_1 >}}

**Speaker**: *{{< meta talk.speaker_1 >}}* \
{{< meta bio.speaker_1 >}}
